entries.headline: Ottawa investigates Chinese miner hiring as B.C. labour rules face scrutiny
entries.summary:
Federal authorities launched an investigation into British Columbia mining companies' recruitment of Chinese miners, examining compliance with Canadian immigration, labour and trade regulations. The inquiry targets foreign labour practices during a period when domestic mining labour remains scarce.

The investigation directly affects B.C. mining operators' ability to recruit international workers during commodity booms when Canadian miners are in short supply. Companies relying on foreign recruitment pipelines may face operational delays or higher labour costs if current practices are deemed non-compliant. Mining operations in the Interior and northern B.C. have increasingly turned to international recruitment to fill specialized positions.

Watch whether the investigation expands to other provinces or mining companies using similar international recruitment strategies.

entries.headline: Trump trade czar keeps Canada tariffs despite continental trade pact
entries.summary:
U.S. President Donald Trump's trade czar announced tariffs on Canadian goods will remain despite the CUSMA continental trade agreement. The statement contradicts the foundational premise of the trade pact designed to eliminate most tariffs between the three North American partners.

This represents a material shift in U.S. trade policy that affects Canadian exporters across automotive, agriculture, energy and advanced manufacturing sectors. Companies shipping goods south now face continued tariff costs that were supposed to be eliminated under CUSMA. Auto parts manufacturers in Ontario, grain farmers in Saskatchewan, and energy producers in Alberta all face higher costs when accessing their largest export market.

Watch whether Canada triggers the dispute resolution mechanism under CUSMA to challenge the tariffs as treaty violations.

entries.headline: Kahnawàːke manufacturer LaFlesche enters Canada's defence supply chain
entries.summary:
From the workshops of LaFlesche in Kahnawàːke, Quebec, precision-manufactured components now flow to some of Canada's largest defence contractors. The company, owned by and based in the Mohawk community across the St. Lawrence River from Montreal, has secured contracts with General Dynamics and CAE, marking a significant milestone for Indigenous participation in Canada's defence industrial base.

Kahnawàːke has long balanced economic development with sovereignty, from the ironworkers who built Manhattan's skyscrapers to today's logistics hub serving Montreal. The community of roughly 8,000 people has developed sophisticated manufacturing capabilities, with LaFlesche representing the latest evolution of that industrial expertise.

The announcement, timed ahead of CANSEC 2026, Canada's premier defence trade show, positions LaFlesche as a case study in Indigenous business excellence within strategic sectors. General Dynamics and CAE's decision to source from LaFlesche reflects both the company's technical capabilities and growing intentionality in Canadian defence procurement to diversify suppliers.

For Kahnawàːke, LaFlesche's defence contracts represent economic sovereignty in action—the community controlling its participation in sectors that matter to Canadian national security. The precision manufacturing required for defence applications demands exactly the kind of high-skill, high-wage employment that Indigenous communities have long sought to develop on their own terms.

This development signals broader momentum in Indigenous economic participation in strategic sectors, moving beyond resource extraction toward advanced manufacturing and technology integration.

entries.headline: Saskatchewan steel faces federal rescue as tariff pressure mounts
entries.summary:
Saskatchewan's steel and manufacturing heartland is buckling under tariff pressure, prompting Ottawa to deploy $8.1 million in emergency support for the province's metal industries. The funding acknowledges what plant floors in Regina and Saskatoon already know—trade friction is squeezing margins in capital-intensive sectors that employ thousands.

The province's steel sector, concentrated around Regina's industrial corridor and Saskatoon's manufacturing district, faces a double squeeze. U.S. tariffs on steel imports hit Saskatchewan producers directly, while downstream tariffs on manufactured goods reduce demand from Canadian manufacturers who buy Saskatchewan steel. Plant managers report order delays and pricing pressure as customers seek alternatives.

John Peterson, a steelworker at Regina's Ipsco plant, sees the impact daily. "We're running two shifts instead of three," he says. "Orders that used to flow from Minneapolis and Chicago are drying up because of the tariff costs." Similar patterns emerge at smaller manufacturers throughout the province's industrial base.

This federal intervention reflects Ottawa's recognition that prairie manufacturing—often overshadowed by agriculture—employs significant numbers in urban centres like Regina and Saskatoon. Saskatchewan's steel and manufacturing sectors provide industrial diversification that provincial leaders have pursued for decades.

The practical takeaway: Saskatchewan's manufacturing workers and communities should expect continued federal support as long as trade tensions persist, but the underlying competitiveness challenge requires either tariff resolution or permanent industrial policy shifts.
